notes: Run 2026-06-22 (category 2 of 2). Active count was 31 — second-thinnest eligible category. Focused heavily on the under-weight $1000 tier (only 1 active). Fills missing Literary Characters subcategory (0 active), thin Children's & YA (1 active), thin Awards/Movements/Terms (1 active). Draws from Dickens, Poirot, Carroll, Shakespeare, Hardy, Fitzgerald, Twain, Tolstoy, Eliot, Defoe, Dumas docs.

questions.clue: The greatest novelist of the Victorian era by many accounts, this English author left school at 12 when his father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a boot-blacking factory.

citations.title: Charles Dickens
citations.snippet: Charles John Huffam Dickens was an English writer and journalist. He created some of literature's best-known fictional characters, and is regarded by many as the greatest novelist of the Victorian era. His works enjoyed unprecedented popula

questions.clue: Agatha Christie's most famous and longest-running fictional creation, this fastidious Belgian detective relies on his 'little grey cells' and appears in 33 novels and 51 short stories.

citations.title: Hercule Poirot
citations.snippet: Hercule Poirot is a recurring fictional Belgian detective created by the English writer Agatha Christie. Poirot is Christie's most famous and longest-running character, appearing in 33 novels, two plays and 51 short stories published betwee

questions.clue: An example of the literary nonsense genre, this 1865 Lewis Carroll children's novel follows a young girl who tumbles down a rabbit hole into a fantasy world of anthropomorphic creatures.

citations.title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
citations.snippet: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland is an 1865 English children's novel by Lewis Carroll. It tells the story of a little girl named Alice who falls through a rabbit hole into a fantasy world of anthropomorphic creatures. It is seen as an examp

questions.clue: Written around 1603, this Shakespeare tragedy is set in Venice and Cyprus and depicts a Moorish military commander manipulated by his ensign into suspecting his wife of infidelity.
aliases: The Tragedy of Othello, the Moor of Venice

questions.clue: Though he saw himself primarily as a poet, this English Victorian author achieved fame through his novels set in the fictional county of Wessex, including 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles.'

citations.title: Thomas Hardy

questions.clue: The American novelist who coined the term 'Jazz Age' in a 1922 short story collection achieved transient success in the 1920s but received critical acclaim only after his death.

citations.title: F. Scott Fitzgerald
citations.snippet: Francis Scott Key Fitzgerald, widely known as F. Scott Fitzgerald or simply Scott Fitzgerald, was an American novelist, essayist, and short story writer. He is best known for his novels depicting the flamboyance and excess of the Jazz Age,

questions.clue: This direct sequel to 'Tom Sawyer' was among the first major American novels written entirely in vernacular English; it is also a satire on antebellum attitudes, particularly racism.

citations.title: Adventures of Huckleberry Finn

questions.clue: Leo Tolstoy himself declared this 1869 epic 'not a novel, even less is it a poem, and still less a historical chronicle'; it follows Russian aristocratic families through Napoleon's invasion.

citations.title: War and Peace

questions.clue: Published in eight paper-bound installments from 1871 to 1872, this Victorian novel was written by Mary Ann Evans — the woman who adopted the male pen name George Eliot to be taken seriously.

citations.title: Middlemarch
citations.snippet: Middlemarch, A Study of Provincial Life is a novel by English author George Eliot, the pen name of Mary Ann Evans. It appeared in eight paper-bound instalments from December 1871 to December 1872, with a book version in four volumes publish

questions.clue: Daniel Defoe's 1719 novel — often credited as the first English work of realistic fiction — follows a man stranded on a tropical island near Venezuela for 28 years.

citations.title: Robinson Crusoe

questions.clue: In this Shakespeare comedy, the fairy queen Titania and king Oberon are quarreling over an Indian changeling boy, while four young Athenian lovers stumble through the fairies' forest.

citations.title: A Midsummer Night's Dream

questions.clue: Though titled for a famous group of royal guards, this 1844 Dumas novel was composed with ghostwriter Auguste Maquet and centers on the young Gascon d'Artagnan, who longs to join their ranks.

citations.title: The Three Musketeers

questions.clue: Shakespeare adapted this tragedy from William Painter's 1567 prose retelling 'Palace of Pleasure,' which itself drew on a 1562 English verse translation of a novella by Italian writer Matteo Bandello.
aliases: The Tragedy of Romeo and Juliet
